title: practical english usage - Part 163
content:
Even in a very formal style, prepositions are not often put at the beginning of and that.

Note that after prepositions which and whom can be used, but not normally who It is a boring place in which to live.

She needs other children with whom to play.

pronoun is used.

This can also happen in infinitive complements, in a very formal style. A  relative She was the only person with whom he could discuss his problems.

It was the house about which he had told them.

With whom did she go?

structures, before the question word or relative pronoun.

In a more formal style, a preposition is often put earlier in questions and relative 7 formal     structures:       With      whom       .. .?

Since when have you been working for her? (     When have you been working not During which period did it happen? (     Which period did it happen during?) During and since are not normally put at the ends of clauses.

6 exceptions:       During       which       period       .. .?

Their house isn't easy to get to.

I've got lots of music to listen to.

Can you get me a chair to stand on?

She needs other children to play with.

The village is pleasant to live in.

Infinitive complements (►   101-102)  can have prepositions with them.

5  infinitive       structures:       pleasant         to   live    in Kate was operated on last night.

I don't know where he is - his bed hasn't been slept in.

She likes to be looked at.

In passive structures (►  Section 6), prepositions go with their verbs.

4 passives:     She     likes     to   be   looked        at Section18 Prepositions grammar •  210   prepositions before conjunctions does it.) I'm not sure how he does it. (More natural than I'm not sure of how he - I'm not sure of his method.

We may be late - it depends (on) how much  traffic there is.

- We may be late - it depends on the traffic.

whether she believed in God.) I asked her whether she believed in God. (More natural than I asked her about - I asked her about her religious beliefs.

Tell me (about) where you went.

- Tell me about your trip.

question words. This is especially common in indirect questions. Compare: prepositions can be dropped before who, which, what, where, whether and other After some very common words like tell, ask, depend, sure, idea, look, 4 question       words not (     . . . responsible (for) that the child had run away.) He said the parents were responsible for the fact that the child had run away.

home. (    The judge paid a lol of attention (to) that the child...) The judge paid a lot of attention to the fact that the child was unhappy at and that.

the expression the fact (►  264.3) is generally put between the preposition reactions) prepositions cannot so often be dropped before that-c lauses. Instead, In other cases (not involving indirect speech or words referring to emotional 3 the    fact     that not I was surprised that she was so strong. (     .. . surprised at that she was...) - I was surprised at her strength.

We are sorry that the train is late. (     . .. sorry about that the train is late.) - We are sorry about the delay.

to emotional reactions. Compare: Prepositions are also dropped before that after many common words that refer 2 emotional        reactions:      prepositions         dropped I wasn't aware that it was so late. (NOT    l wasn't aware of that it was so late.) - I wasn't aware of the time.

She had no idea that I was unhappy. (     She had no idea of that - She had no idea of my state of mind.

I knew that he had problems. (     I knew about that he had problems.) - I knew about his problems.

usually dropped before that-c lauses. Compare: speech, after words that refer to saying, writing, thinking, etc, prepositions are Prepositions are not used directly before the conjunction that. In indirect 1  indirect      speech:     prepositions         dropped       before     that Prepositions can be followed by conjunctions in some cases but not in others.

210 prepositions         before     conjunctions        _______ grammar «212     prepositions before particular words and expressions What's on at the cinema this week?                    -> at the cinema (BrE); at the theatre; at a party; at university preposition + noun combinations, see a good dictionary.
